title: The Modes
subtitle: A small finite state machine you carry in your head
summary: *Normal*, *insert*, *visual*, *replace*, *command-line*, *operator-pending*, *terminal*. What each is for and how to move between them.

blocks.text: Vim has seven modes. You will spend essentially all your time in two of them — *normal* and *insert*. The other five matter for specific jobs. Knowing they exist, and how the transitions work, is more important than memorizing every command in each.

rows: Select text, then act on it.
Run an Ex command or search.
*(empty, but last key was an operator)*
Waiting for a motion or text object.

blocks.text: From any mode except *normal mode*, {key:Esc} returns to *normal mode*. From *insert* and *visual mode*, {key:Ctrl-[} does the same. Build the {key:Esc} reflex: after every thought, escape.
*Terminal mode* is the exception — {key:Esc} is forwarded to the shell. Use {key:Ctrl-\}{key:Ctrl-N} to escape from a Vim terminal.

If you want a visual cue for what mode you're in, configure your status line to show it. Most users learn the mode by feel — but for the first week, having `[INSERT]` or `[NORMAL]` in the corner removes a lot of confusion.

When you press an operator like {key:d}, {key:c}, {key:y}, or {key:>}, Vim enters *operator-pending mode* and waits for a motion or text object. You're in *normal mode* no longer — keys do different things in this state. {key:i} no longer enters *insert mode*; it begins a text object (inner ...). {key:a} no longer appends; it begins around-text-objects. The operator and motion together form a single change, which is what {key:.} repeats.

blocks.label: Operator d → operator-pending → text object iw → execute

blocks.caption: Vim's seven modes and the transitions between them. *Normal mode* is the hub: every other mode is one keystroke away, and {key:Esc} always returns. *Visual mode* has three sub-modes — char, line, block — switched between by pressing {key:v} / {key:V} / {key:Ctrl-V} again while already in *visual mode*. *Terminal mode* is the one place {key:Esc} doesn't escape: use {key:Ctrl-\}{key:Ctrl-N}.

blocks.title: The {key:Ctrl-O} Trick
blocks.text: The most useful transition not obvious from the diagram is {key:Ctrl-O} from *insert mode*: it pops you into *normal mode* for *exactly one command*, then automatically returns. So {key:Ctrl-O}{key:d}{key:d} from *insert mode* deletes the current line and continues typing where the new cursor lands -- without the round trip of {key:Esc}, then {key:d}{key:d}, then {key:i}.

blocks.title: Per-Mode State
blocks.text:
Each mode carries its own state. *Normal mode* tracks the count being typed ({key:5}{key:j} is two keystrokes; the {key:5} is buffered). *Insert mode* buffers the inserted text so it can be repeated with {key:.} and so {key:Ctrl-O} can briefly leave and return. *Visual mode* tracks the *anchor* (where you started) and the *cursor* (where you are now); the selection is the range between them, recomputed every keystroke. *Operator-pending mode* tracks which operator is pending, plus its count and any selected register.

When the mode ends, this state is either committed (*insert* → *normal* commits the typed text and the count's repetitions; *operator-pending* → *normal* applies the operator to the motion's range) or discarded (*visual* → *normal* without an operator just throws the selection away, though {key:gv} can recover it from the {key:'<} / {key:'>} marks).

When something surprises you in Vim — a key doesn't do what you expected — nine times out of ten you misjudged the current mode. "I pressed {key:dd} and it just typed two `d`s" → you were in *insert mode*. "I pressed {key:i} and a word disappeared" → you were in *operator-pending mode* after {key:d}. The fix is always the same: press {key:Esc} (sometimes twice) and start over from *normal mode*.
